title: Soy wax tealights
subtitle: Twelve refillable tealights in soy wax with optional essential oil
excerpt: Twelve soy wax tealights using refillable metal tealight cups. Each tealight burns 4-5 hours. The fragrance load is optional and low; tealights burn quickly so the heat throw matters more than the cold throw.
sourceNotes: CandleScience tealight-making guide (candlescience.com, May 2026), tealight wax weight per cup, wick sizing and fragrance load notes.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale linearly; 250 g of wax fills roughly 12-14 tealight cups.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Tealights are ready to burn once fully set, approximately 2 hours after pouring.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Effectively indefinite. Soy wax does not degrade. Fragrance fades after 1-2 years; replace wax to refresh.

content.text: A batch of twelve soy wax tealights. Each tealight cup holds approximately 20 g of wax and burns 4-5 hours. Tealights are the fastest natural-home candle project: one session pours a fortnight's supply, and refillable aluminium tealight cups eliminate the single-use plastic.

Fragrance load for tealights is lower than jar candles; 4-5% of wax weight is standard. Because tealights burn quickly, the hot throw (scent when burning) is more important than the cold throw. Adding fragrance is optional; unscented tealights are completely functional and a safer default around pets.
Melt wax on a simmering-water bath, not directly on the hob. Never leave melting wax unattended. Keep a kitchen fire blanket within reach.

items.prepNote: container-grade soy wax. 240 g fills approximately 12 standard tealight cups at 20 g per cup.
optional. 4% of wax weight (approximately 10 g ≈ 10 ml). Or substitute any single essential oil. Add at 55°C.
tealight-size pre-tabbed wicks. The tab keeps the wick at the base centre of the cup; the wick length is approximately 1-1.5 cm above the wax surface.

content.text: Set out the tealight cups and wicks

Place 12 tealight cups on a flat, level surface. Press one pre-tabbed wick into each cup so the tab sits flat on the base centre. The wick needs no additional adhesive; the wax poured around it will hold it in place.

Place the soy wax in the pouring pitcher and set it in a saucepan of simmering water. Stir gently as the wax melts. Remove from the heat when fully liquid. Allow the wax to cool; when the probe reads 55°C, stir in the essential oil if using. When the wax reaches 60-65°C, it is ready to pour.

Pour the wax steadily into each tealight cup, filling to within 2 mm of the rim. Soy wax in small volumes sets quickly; work through all 12 cups in one pass. The wicks will be pulled slightly off-centre by the wax flow; straighten them by hand while the wax is still soft if needed. Leave undisturbed for 2 hours until fully set. Trim each wick to 5 mm before the first burn.

Effectively indefinite. Store tealights in a small tin or box away from dust and direct sunlight. When the wax is spent, push the used wick tab out of the base of the metal cup, wipe clean, and refill using this recipe. The metal cups last many refill cycles.
